3.2.4. Spectra of velocity ﬂuctuations in ﬂames
To the best of the authors’ knowledge, Ballal [251] was the ﬁrst
to document the inﬂuence of combustion on the spectrum of
velocity ﬂuctuations. He compared the energy spectra measured on
the axis of a conﬁned V-shaped ﬂame (i.e. in products) with the
corresponding spectra in the cold ﬂow. For weakly turbulent ﬂames
characterized by u0 < 2SL and h > dL, a decrease (increase) in the
former spectra at high (low) wavenumbers was reported, see Fig. 4
in the cited paper.
Go¨kalp et al. [211] applied a single-component LDA system to
obtain velocity spectra from two V-shape ﬂames characterized by
the same Ret ¼ 110, but different st ¼ L/u0 (st ¼ 19 and 7 in ﬂames A
and B, respectively). The spectra in the reactants, products, and in
the ﬂame brush were recorded by changing the measurement
points. For ﬂame B, the average slopes of the spectra obtained from
products were scattered around the value of 5/3 (see Table III in the
cited paper), which is associated with the Kolmogorov spectrum.
For ﬂame A, the average slopes of the spectra obtained from
products were substantially higher (see Table II therein) than 5/3. It
is worth noting that Fig. 3 from the cited paper

title: Effects of premixed ﬂames on turbulence and turbulent scalar transport
text:
3.2.4. Spectra of velocity ﬂuctuations in ﬂames
To the best of the authors’ knowledge, Ballal [251] was the ﬁrst
to document the inﬂuence of combustion on the spectrum of
velocity ﬂuctuations. He compared the energy spectra measured on
the axis of a conﬁned V-shaped ﬂame (i.e. in products) with the
corresponding spectra in the cold ﬂow. For weakly turbulent ﬂames
characterized by u0 < 2SL and h > dL, a decrease (increase) in the
former spectra at high (low) wavenumbers was reported, see Fig. 4
in the cited paper.
Go¨kalp et al. [211] applied a single-component LDA system to
obtain velocity spectra from two V-shape ﬂames characterized by
the same Ret ¼ 110, but different st ¼ L/u0 (st ¼ 19 and 7 in ﬂames A
and B, respectively). The spectra in the reactants, products, and in
the ﬂame brush were recorded by changing the measurement
points. For ﬂame B, the average slopes of the spectra obtained from
products were scattered around the value of 5/3 (see Table III in the
cited paper), which is associated with the Kolmogorov spectrum.
For ﬂame A, the average slopes of the spectra obtained from
products were substantially higher (see Table II therein) than 5/3. It
is worth noting that Fig. 3 from the cited paper shows that, even for
ﬂame A, the slope is about 5/3 in a narrow frequency range.
The u-spectra measured by Videto and Santavicca [245] in the
vicinity of the ﬂame brush showed departures from the Kolmo-
gorov scaling both in reactants (the mean slope is 1.4) and products
(the mean slope decreases from 2.7 to 2.2 with distance from the
ﬂame), see Fig. 11 in the cited paper. However, inspection of the
spectra obtained from the reactants in the vicinity of the ﬂame and
from the products far from the ﬂame (see curves 2 and 4 in the
discussed ﬁgure) reveals narrow frequency ranges characterized by
the Kolmogorov slope of 5/3. Such a range is not observed in the
spectrum obtained from the products in the vicinity of the ﬂame
(curve 3), where a local maximum of the power sp

title: Effects of premixed ﬂames on turbulence and turbulent scalar transport
text:
Heitor et al. [244] have reported countergradient axial ﬂux rv00c00
but gradient transverse ﬂux ru00c00 in a lean (F ¼ 0.79) methane–air
ﬂame stabilized by the recirculation zone behind a disk bafﬂe.
Similar results were obtained by Veynante et al. [135] from
a conﬁned, nearly stoichiometric propane–air, V-shaped ﬂame at
a far distance from the ﬂame holder, whereas not only ru00c00 but
also rv00c00 were gradient in the vicinity of the holder.
Most et al. [220] and Pfadler et al. [240] have also showed that
the direction of turbulent scalar ﬂux changes from gradient to
countergradient within the ﬂame brush (e.g. see Fig. 48 in Section
5.1). Surprisingly, the countergradient axial ﬂux rv00c00 measured by
Most et al. [220] was more pronounced (i) in a highly turbulent (u0/
SL ¼ 12) lean (F ¼ 0.7) methane–air ﬂame stabilized by a bluff body
than in a weakly turbulent (u0/SL ¼ 0.5) lean (F ¼ 0.7) methane–air
ﬂame stabilized by a rod, and (ii) in a leaner (F ¼ 0.7) rather than in
a richer (F ¼ 0.8) methane–air ﬂame stabilized by the bluff body. To
the contrary, the magnitude of ru00c00 obtained by Pfadler et al. [240]
from swirl-stabilized V-shaped methane–air ﬂames was increased
by F in the range of F ¼ 0.67–0.83.
Troiani et al. [246] have documented countergradient scalar
ﬂux from near-stoichiometric methane–air ﬂames, whereas ru00c00
was gradient in a lean ﬂame (F ¼ 0.67) characterized by a lower
sSL/u0.
Countergradient scalar ﬂux was also found in many DNS. For
instance, Rutland and Cant [270] have reported that ub > uu > 0.
Swaminathan et al. [267] obtained countergradient ﬂux ru00c00 by
processing the DNS database by Zhang and Rutland [271]. The DNS
by Boger et al. [274] yielded gradient and countergradient normal
scalar ﬂuxes at s   2 and s ¼ 6, respectively. Nishiki et al. [280] tried
to obtain fully developed turbulent ﬂames in DNS and reported
countergradient normal scalar ﬂux even at s as low as 1.5.
The DNS data reported by Veynante et al. [128] indicate a tra

title: Effects of premixed ﬂames on turbulence and turbulent scalar transport
text:
experimental [129,130,132,135,220,240] and DNS [128,131,274,295]
studies by varying either the density ratio [274], or the normalized
rms turbulent velocity u0/SL [128–132], or the distance from the
ﬂame holder [135,220,240,295], or ﬂame development time [128].
This problem will be further discussed in Section 5.1.
Another interesting issue is how strong countergradient scalar
transport can be. Since, according to the BML Eq. (47), the magni-
tude of scalar ﬂux ru00c00 is proportional to the magnitude of the slip
velocity, the issue may be reformulated as follows: How large can
the slip velocity be in premixed turbulent ﬂames?

Cheng and Shepherd [174] from three stoichiometric methane–air
ﬂames stabilized by either a rod (V-shaped), or a rim (Bunsen), or
a wall (impinging jet) in turbulent ﬂows that had almost the same
characteristics at the burner exit. The values of DU/(sSL) depend on
c, ﬂame type, and the point of measurement, but in all the cases, the
normalized slip velocity was lower than unity. For the stoichio-
metric ethylene–air ﬂame s9 stabilized in an impinging jet and

Cho et al. [224] have reported the normalized slip velocities

and no clear dependence of the slip velocity on the mean

Cheng [233] has investigated a number of swirl-stabilized
ﬂames and has found that DU/(sSL) z 0.6 for c < 0:5 but the data
are ‘‘more scattered for c > 0:5.’’
The data obtained by Gulati and Driscoll [241, 242, 243] from
stoichiometric methane–air oblique unconﬁned ﬂames show that
DU/(sSL) is markedly less than unity, but this ratio is higher for
a leaner ﬂame (F ¼ 0.8) and may be close to unity for a sufﬁciently
lean (F ¼ 0.7) ﬂame.
Pfadler et al. [240] obtained DU/(sSL) z 0.67 from a swirl-stabi-
lized, V-shaped, moderately lean (F ¼ 0.83) methane–air ﬂame.
The ratio of DU/(sSL) was about 0.2 in recent experiments with
a slightly rich methane–air ﬂame stabilized by a bluff body [246].
The DNS by Rutland and Cant [270] has yielded DU/(sSL) z 0.3.
The DNS by Chakraborty and Cant [289] has shown (see Fig. 3e in
the cited paper) that DU/(sSL) is about unity for Le ¼ 0.6, but may be
as large as three for Le ¼ 0.34. For Le  0.8 this ratio is negative
(gradient transport) under the conditions of their DNS.
Some authors did not evaluate slip velocity, but reported the
dependencies of the turbulent scalar ﬂux on the combustion
progress variable. To estimate DU and DV using such data, we
applied the BML Eqs. (44) and (47) to calculate the slip velocity
associated with the maximum values of jru00c00j and jrv00c00j,
respectively. The results were as follows:
For the lean hydrogen–air Bunsen ﬂames studied by Chen and
Bilger [196], DU/(sSL) ¼ 0.5 if F ¼ 0.45 (sSL ¼ 1.7 m/s), DU/(sSL) ¼ 0.3
if F ¼ 0.6 (sSL ¼ 5.2 m/s), DU/(sSL) ¼ 0.24–0.4 (depending on the
incident turbulence) if F ¼ 0.7 (sSL ¼ 8 m/s). In the ﬂames investi-
gated by Most et al. [220], DV/(sSL) was commonly less than unity,
but slip velocities higher (than sSL) were also recorded at large c.
For ﬂames studied in DNS, DU/(sSL) ¼ 0.7 [271, 267], DU/
(sSL) ¼ 0.2 [274], DU/(sSL)   1.3, 0.67, and 0.33 if u00/SL ¼ 2, 3, and 5,
respectively [128], DU/(sSL)   1.3 if u00/SL ¼ 5 and a strong pressure
gradient was imposed to promote countergradient transport [131],
DU/(sSL) ¼ 0.8, 0.6, and 0.4 if sSL ¼ 3.9, 2.1, and 0.62 m/s, respec-
tively [280]. The results of the DNS of V-shaped ﬂames, reported by
Domingo et al. [295], show that DU/(sSL) is much less than DV/
(sSL) < 0.5.
For ﬂames stabilized in impinging jets, Li et al. [227] and Stevens
et al. [228] have reported axial proﬁles of ru00c00=r. Invoking the BML
Eq. (47) and associating the peak values of this second moment
with the peak value of ~cð1  ~cÞ, we have estimated DU/(sSL) ¼ 0.8 if
sSL ¼ 1.9 m/s
[227],

if

[228],

(sSL) ¼ 0.7 if sSL ¼ 2.4 m/s [228], and DU/(sSL) ¼ 0.4 if sSL ¼ 1.8 m/s
[228].
All in all, the above data show that if scalar transport is coun-
tergradient, then the slip velocity is typically of the order of, but less
than, sSL. In conﬁned ﬂames, however, the slip velocity may be
substantially higher under the inﬂuence of the mean pressure
gradient. For instance, Shepherd et al. [257] have reported
DV > 5 m/s for a rich F ¼ 1.2 propane–air ﬂame characterized by
sSL ¼ 2.7 m/s.
The above discussion focuses on the turbulent transport of
a scalar that characterizes a burning mixture. Combustion can also
affect
other
turbulent
ﬂuxes.

instance,
Cheng
et
al.
[136,206,207] have documented the so-called negative turbulent
viscosity in V-shaped ﬂames, i.e. both u0v0ðvu=vyÞ and u0v0ðvv=vxÞ are
positive so that term I in Eq. (23) serves to decrease the ATKE.

Similar to countergradient scalar ﬂux, large positive values of the
mean Reynolds stress u0v0 were controlled by the slip velocity in
these experiments, see the intermittency term in Eq. (49). Negative
turbulent viscosity has also been reported by Heitor et al. [244].
Driscoll and Gulati [243] have measured the second ru02 and
third ru03 moments in an oblique unconﬁned ﬂame and have found
that the sign of the latter moment indicates the ﬂux of the ATKE
toward ~c ¼ 0:6 associated with the peak value of the former
moment. They called this phenomenon the ‘‘countergradient
diffusion of TKE.’’
3.4. Summary
The experimental and DNS data reviewed above clearly show
that premixed combustion can substantially affect both uncondi-
tioned (stronger) and conditioned (weaker) moments of a ﬂuctu-
ating velocity ﬁeld and reverse the direction of turbulent scalar ﬂux
at least if the incident turbulence is sufﬁciently weak, i.e. a ratio of
u0/(sSL) is low.
Such effects are commonly more pronounced (i) in

title: Effects of premixed ﬂames on turbulence and turbulent scalar transport
text:
Similar to countergradient scalar ﬂux, large positive values of the
mean Reynolds stress u0v0 were controlled by the slip velocity in
these experiments, see the intermittency term in Eq. (49). Negative
turbulent viscosity has also been reported by Heitor et al. [244].
Driscoll and Gulati [243] have measured the second ru02 and
third ru03 moments in an oblique unconﬁned ﬂame and have found
that the sign of the latter moment indicates the ﬂux of the ATKE
toward ~c ¼ 0:6 associated with the peak value of the former
moment. They called this phenomenon the ‘‘countergradient
diffusion of TKE.’’
3.4. Summary
The experimental and DNS data reviewed above clearly show
that premixed combustion can substantially affect both uncondi-
tioned (stronger) and conditioned (weaker) moments of a ﬂuctu-
ating velocity ﬁeld and reverse the direction of turbulent scalar ﬂux
at least if the incident turbulence is sufﬁciently weak, i.e. a ratio of
u0/(sSL) is low.
Such effects are commonly more pronounced (i) in the velocity-
vector component that is closer to the normal to the ﬂame brush,
(ii) in weakly turbulent ﬂows, and (iii) in mixtures characterized by
a higher density ratio and a higher laminar ﬂame speed. Particular
features of the inﬂuence of premixed combustion on turbulence are
very sensitive to ﬂame type (ﬂow geometry, stabilization mecha-
nism, conﬁned or not, etc.), incident turbulence and mixture
characteristics, the point of measurement or the time after ignition
(e.g. in unsteady DNS), etc.
The high values of the unconditioned moments (u02, v02, k, u0v0,
etc.) well documented in various weakly turbulent premixed
ﬂames are mainly caused by the unburned–burned intermittency.
The transition from gradient to countergradient transport has
been observed in many experimental [129,130,132,135,220,240]
and DNS [128,131,274,295] studies by varying either the density
ratio [274], or the normalized rms turbulent velocity u0/SL [128–
132], or distance from the ﬂame holder [135,220,240,2

title: Effects of premixed ﬂames on turbulence and turbulent scalar transport
text:
noting that these results show that (i) the mean dilatation term is
an important sink term, (ii) the mean pressure gradient term is the
main source term, but (iii) the ﬂuctuating pressure gradient plays
a minor role in the ﬂames modeled by Treurniet et al. [142].
All the terms on the RHS of Eq. (21) were ﬁrst evaluated using
DNS by Rutland and Cant [270] and by Trouve´ et al. [127]. In the
former DNS, the ﬂux ru00c00 was countergradient (with the excep-
tion of the leading edge of the ﬂame brush), whereas the latter
DNS yielded gradient behavior. In both cases, the ﬂuctuating
pressure gradient term v played an important role and served to
cause countergradient scalar transport. In the ﬂame studied by
Rutland and Cant [270], the mean pressure gradient term vi was
even more important than term v and also served to promote
countergradient scalar transport. In the ﬂame investigated by
Trouve´ et al. [127], term vi was markedly lower than term v and
acted in the opposite direction during the early stage of ﬂame
development. At later stages, the mean pressure gradient term
changed signs, served to promote countergradient scalar trans-
port, and become larger than term v. All other terms on the RHS of
Eq. (21) were substantially less than the pressure terms v and vi in
the simulations by Rutland and Cant [270], but the production
term i (more precisely, the contribution of the gradient of the
mean combustion progress variable into the production term)
played an important role and served to cause gradient scalar
transport in the DNS by Trouve´ et al. [127].
The DNS by Veynante et al. [128] also emphasized the
important role played by the pressure terms v and vi in Eq. (21).
At u00/SL ¼ 2, the two terms and the reaction term vii served to
promote countergradient scalar transport. The dissipation terms
iii and iv acted in the opposite direction. All ﬁve of the afore-
mentioned terms were of the same order, and the transport term
ii was also signiﬁcant. Thus, in this case, no

term were reported in Ref. [308], probably because only part of the
original database [253–255] (e.g. the part associated with high u0/

Driscoll and Gulati [243] measured all the terms in the balance
Eq. (23) for ~k, with the exception of terms III and IV. The reported

title: Effects of premixed ﬂames on turbulence and turbulent scalar transport
text:
results show that the mean pressure gradient is an important
source of ~k, whereas term I is a sink term due to the mean
dilatation.
In a ﬂame stabilized by a disk bafﬂe, however, the mean pressure
gradient was reported [244] to play a minor role in the total
production of ~k and ru00v00 ‘‘throughout the ﬂow,’’ in part, because
the mean axial pressure gradient changed signs in the middle of the
ﬂame brush. The total production of ~k and ru00v00 was dominated by
the shear-stress term I in this highly sheared ﬂow. Nevertheless,
even in this case, the mean pressure gradient term vi [see Eq. (21)]
was dominant in the balance equation for axial scalar ﬂux and
played an important role in the balance of radial ﬂux. In the latter
case, term vi was balanced by term i and served to promote
gradient transport, because the mean radial pressure gradient was
pointed at reactants. The transport term ii played an important role
in the balance of the axial scalar ﬂux.
Thus, the above DNS and experimental data show that the
pressure-gradient terms v–vi and IV–V play a signiﬁcant role in the
balance equations for the second moments ru00

premixed turbulent ﬂames. However, other unclosed terms in Eqs.
(21), (22), and (23) are not negligible in a general case and, thus, all
these terms need closure. Models developed for this purpose are
reviewed in the next subsections.

Experimental data obtained by Cheng and Shepherd [173]
from V-shaped ﬂames show variations in K3 in the range from
0.011 to 0.102 and do not ‘‘lend strong support to’’ Eq. (78),
which was claimed to ‘‘be satisfactory as a ﬁrst approximation’’
[173]. Anand and Pope [320], however, have reported that their
numerical results obtained by solving a balance equation for
a joint PDF rP(u, c) differ signiﬁcantly from Eq. (78). Measure-

The k–3 model recast into the Favre-averaged form is also widely

premixed ﬂames. First, Borghi and Escudie [333] have pointed out
that since the mean pressure gradient contributes to the production

mean pressure gradient. Accordingly, they modiﬁed the equation as

Second, even if we highlight the FGV observed locally by
Steinberg et al. [202], Driscoll [52] has pointed out that this
vorticity ‘‘is a stabilizing factor that tends to decrease the amplitude
of the wrinkles’’ (e.g. see white arrows, which show the direction of
the rotational ﬂow in the FGV region in Fig. 58). Therefore, this FGV
neither increases the ﬂame-front area nor the turbulent burning
velocity.
6. Conclusions
The present review may very brieﬂy be summarized as follows.
Premixed ﬂames can substantially affect a ﬂuctuating velocity ﬁeld
and both the magnitude and direction of turbulent scalar ﬂuxes, but
we are not yet capable of predicting such effects in a general case,
especially if the upstream rms turbulence velocity u0 and the
laminar ﬂame speed SL are of the same order.
The hypothesis that turbulence generated by combustion
substantially accelerates ﬂame propagation does not seem to be
supported by the experimental and DNS data reviewed in Section 3.
Nevertheless, such a mechanism might be of importance in certain
unstable large-scale ﬂames, e.g. in self-accelerating unconﬁned
spherical ﬂame kernels.
More speciﬁc conclusions are reported in Sections 3.4 and 4.6,
whereas certain important unresolved basic issues are highlighted
in Section 5.
7. Addendum: a physical appraisal
With great care, we have critically reviewed the large amount of
literature dealing with the problem of the inﬂuence of heat release
on turbulence and premixed ﬂame propagation, posed by Scurlock
et al. [53,54] and Karlovitz et al. [55] more than a half century ago.
We have arrived at the conclusion that while the problem is still
very intricate and far from the ﬁnal solution, there is no convincing
evidence that turbulence generated by combustion can substa

title: Effects of premixed ﬂames on turbulence and turbulent scalar transport
text:
Let us consider the limit case of s[1 for the sake of simplicity,
and discuss the ﬂow in the vicinity of the curved ﬂame shown in
Fig. 59. To do so, it is worth using an analogy between the
asymptotic state of the hydrodynamic instability of a bounded
laminar ﬂame and the asymptotic state of the propagation of
a laminar ﬂame in a channel, as drawn by Uberoi [539] ﬁfty years
ago. If we consider a segment of the ﬂame, cut out by straight
streamlines BB0 and CC0, the propagation of this segment, which is
isolated from the inﬂuence of neighboring segments by the straight
streamlines, is representative for the whole ﬂame and is in many
aspects like the propagation of a laminar ﬂame in a channel.
There have been many attempts [539–545] to develop a theory
of a laminar ﬂame in a channel, but without assuming the shape or,
at least, the area of the ﬂame, the problem remains unresolved.
Nevertheless, the basic physics of the problem appears to be quite
clear.
First of all, we know that a laminar ﬂame develops towards
a stationary shape. Then, the ﬂame as a whole moves substantially
faster than the unperturbed laminar ﬂame. For instance, Emmons
[540] has calculated the asymptotically stationary velocity of an
entire ﬂame (s ¼ 4.5) to be equal to 2.5SL.
Second, the stationarity of the ﬂame shape requires the leading
point O to move equally fast despite the fact that the ﬂame is locally
convex towards the unburned mixture and, therefore, the local
speed of the leading point with respect to the mixture is typically
lower than SL (for the Lewis number Le  1). Then, the only possi-
bility for the fast movement of the leading point is a local reduction
in the ﬂow velocity before the leading point, caused by the diver-
gence of the local streamlines (see Fig. 59). The ﬂow divergence is
associated with a high pressure zone developed in the vicinity of
point O. The high pressure zone that pushes the leading point
forward results from the collision of product streamlines generated
by

the symmetry axis in the same cross-section scale respectively as

Here, X is the distance from the leading edge. Therefore, the ampli-
tude of the variations in the axial product velocity and, hence, ufg0 are
decreased by X/AN < 1 and tends to zero as X/0. Furthermore, the
diffusion of turbulence into irrotational ﬂow is controlled by viscous
forces and the turbulent front propagates at a Kolmogorov speed.
Accordingly, the velocity of the diffusion of the ﬂame-generated
turbulence upstream scales as ufg0Ret
1/4 f ufg0(dL/L)1/4 < ufg0. Finally,
the above scaling ufg0 f SLs3/2 appears to overestimate the ﬂame-
generated turbulence velocity, because the production of turbulence
due to the instability of the shear layer is a relatively slow process
accompanied by substantial dissipation of kinetic energy.
The above discussion shows that the inﬂuence of heat release on
ﬂow can accelerate a ﬂame due to pressure forces generated by
vorticity, but the magnitude of the effect is limited by the velocity
OðSL
ﬃﬃﬃs
p Þ. Moreover, the effect is further reduced by the physical
mechanisms discussed in Section 5.3, i.e. damping of ﬂamelet
instabilities by turbulent stretching provided by larger-scale eddies
and a low rate of the growth of unstable perturbations. All in all, the
ﬂame-generated hydrodynamic effects, particularly the increase in
the ﬂame speed caused by heat release, are of secondary impor-
tance if SL  u0.

4.1.4.5. Pressure diffusion. The importance of the pressure-diffu-
sion term on the RHS of Eq. (129) has been demonstrated by Rut-
land and Cant [270], Zhang and Rutland [271], Nishiki et al.
[278,279], and Treurniet et al. [142] in the DNS of statistically
planar, 1D ﬂames. For instance, Fig. 10 in Ref. [271] shows that
pressure diffusion contributes substantially to the xx-component of
the ﬂuctuating velocity–pressure gradient tensor on the LHS of Eq.
(128). In these DNS, the pressure diffusion dominates at low ~c < 0:2
and is comparable to the sum of the redistribution and pressure
dilatation terms at large ~c > 0:6. Thus, although pressure diffusion
does not affect the net balance of the ATKE, it may play an impor-
tant role in the transport of energy within the ﬂame brush and,
thus, should not be neglected in combustion models. For instance,
the same DNS results (see Fig. 26) indicate that pressure diffusion
overwhelms other contributions to the transport term vT 1kk=vx in
the balance equation for ~k and cannot be closed invoking the
approximation of gradient transport, such as Eq. (75).
The present authors are not aware of any model aimed at
evaluating the pressure-diffusion term in ﬂames.

Since term 4 is commonly assumed to play a substantial role in

turbulence) production in ﬂames, this assumption can easily be put
into question.

and without the ﬁrst term on the RHS (which was assumed to be

title: Effects of premixed ﬂames on turbulence and turbulent scalar transport
text:
Their DNS data agree quite well with this approximation (see
Fig. 25) if Civ ¼ 0.34.
4.1.4.5. Pressure diffusion. The importance of the pressure-diffu-
sion term on the RHS of Eq. (129) has been demonstrated by Rut-
land and Cant [270], Zhang and Rutland [271], Nishiki et al.
[278,279], and Treurniet et al. [142] in the DNS of statistically
planar, 1D ﬂames. For instance, Fig. 10 in Ref. [271] shows that
pressure diffusion contributes substantially to the xx-component of
the ﬂuctuating velocity–pressure gradient tensor on the LHS of Eq.
(128). In these DNS, the pressure diffusion dominates at low ~c < 0:2
and is comparable to the sum of the redistribution and pressure
dilatation terms at large ~c > 0:6. Thus, although pressure diffusion
does not affect the net balance of the ATKE, it may play an impor-
tant role in the transport of energy within the ﬂame brush and,
thus, should not be neglected in combustion models. For instance,
the same DNS results (see Fig. 26) indicate that pressure diffusion
overwhelms other contributions to the transport term vT 1kk=vx in
the balance equation for ~k and cannot be closed invoking the
approximation of gradient transport, such as Eq. (75).
The present authors are not aware of any model aimed at
evaluating the pressure-diffusion term in ﬂames.

title: Effects of premixed ﬂames on turbulence and turbulent scalar transport
text:
a and b and, therefore, substantial errors in evaluating W. Errors in
computing ~c and rc002 are inevitable, not only for numerical reasons,
but also and mainly because of the limitations of the physical
models invoked to close the RHSs of Eqs. (18) and (20).
In other words, a too small quantity g cannot be determined
accurately by subtracting one ﬁnite quantity from another ﬁnite
quantity if the two quantities are calculated even with small errors.
Therefore, the mean reaction rate, which is equal to the probability
g multiplied by the reaction rate integrated over the ﬂame front,
cannot be determined accurately too.
The b-function approach may be promising if the probability g is
not too small. For instance, Chen and Bilger [194] have reported that
the b function calculated using measured values of ~c and rc002 agree
reasonably well with the PDFs P(c) obtained from six different
Bunsen ﬂames characterized by g z 0.2 in the middle of the ﬂame
brushes. Mantel and Bilger [266] have drawn a similar conclusion by
processing the DNS database by Trouve´ and Poinsot [265], associated
with the substantial probability g [the parameter g evaluated using
Eq. (70) was as low as 0.5]. To the contrary, Jin et al. [364] have
reported substantial differences between the b-function approxi-
mation and the PDF P(c) obtained by processing DNS data.

4.1.7. Applications and validation
Certain
applications
of
the
models
discussed
above
are
summarized in Table 3.
Bray and Libby [98] applied Eqs. (16)–(18), (20), and (23) to
a statistically planar, 1D, oblique ﬂame. The dissipation term III
scaled as r~k3=2=L. Th

title: Effects of premixed ﬂames on turbulence and turbulent scalar transport
text:
results show that if the angle 4 between the ﬂame and the
upstream ﬂow direction is sufﬁciently small, the production of
ATKE through shear stress exceeds the degradation of ~k due to
dilatation, and the ﬂame may increase the ATKE.
Libby and Bray [100] extended the above model by analyzing
various dependencies of turbulent diffusivity and scalar dissipation
rate on the mean density. For certain dependence, the computed
angle 4 was comparable with the data obtained by Wright and
Zukoski [366] from oblique conﬁned ﬂames stabilized behind
a bluff body. Note that the simulations show a weak dependence of
4 on s in line with the experiments.
Libby and Bray [104] applied Eqs. (16)–(18), (21), and (22) to
a statistically planar, 1D, ﬂame normal to the mean ﬂow. The
pressure terms v in Eq. (21) and IV in Eq. (22) were neglected. The
transport terms ii and II were closed using Eqs. (51) and (52) sup-
plemented with Eqs. (76), (77), and ðu03Þu ¼ ðu03Þb ¼ 0. The dissi-
pation terms iii and iv were modeled using Eq. (102) and the
dissipation term III was modeled using Eq. (101). The reaction term
was closed using Eq. (169) with fn ¼ 5/6. Countergradient scalar
ﬂux was predicted in these simulations.
Bray et al. [105] modiﬁed this model by replacing Eq. (77) with
Eq. (78). By adjusting three model constants, K1 ¼0.85, K2 ¼ 0.25,
and K3 ¼ 0.1, these authors obtained dependencies of ru002ð~cÞ and
ru00c00ð~cÞ which were close to the data measured by Moss [125] (cf.
dashed lines and symbols in Fig. 30). In particular, CGD and an
increase in ru002 with ~c were predicted.
Driscoll and Gulati [243] tested ‘‘exactly the same’’ model by
comparing the proﬁles of ru002ð~cÞ, ru003ð~cÞ, uuð~cÞ, ubð~cÞ, ðu02Þuð~cÞ,
ðu02Þbð~cÞ, ru00c00ð~cÞ, u00ð~cÞ, and ru002c00ð~cÞ computed for a statistically
stationary, planar, 1D ﬂame with the experimental data obtained
from unconﬁned, oblique, methane–air, turbulent ﬂames. For most
proﬁles, the qualitative agreement between the measured and
A.N. Lipatnikov, J. Chomiak / Progress in Energy and Combustion Science 36 (2010) 1–102
49

dicted; (i) values of ru002 in the burned mixture higher than in the
unburned mixture, (ii) an increase in ru002 by s, (iii) the existence of
peak values of ru002 within the ﬂame brush, (iv) an increase in uu
and ub when either ~c or s increases, (v) an increase in ðu02Þu with ~c,
(vi) countergradient scalar ﬂux ru00c00 and an increase in its
magnitude
by
s,

form
of

agreement was even obtained between certain measured and

since the measured mean ﬂame brush thickness increased with
distance from the stabilizing rod in the experiments [243], the
agreement between the results of computations of a statistically
stationary, planar, 1D ﬂame with the data measured at a particular
distance (i.e. at a particular stage of ﬂame development) does not
seem to be a reliable validation.

Moreover, the model yielded an increase in ðu02Þb with ~c at
0:1 < ~c < 0:8, whereas the measurements indicated the opposite
trend. To resolve the problem, Driscoll and Gulati [243] measured
all terms in Eqs. (78)–(80) associated with the statistically planar
1D turbulent ﬂame, and found that Eq. (78), which was invoked in
the discussed computations, and Eq. (80) were contradicted by the
experiment, whereas Eq. (79) was ‘‘signiﬁcantly better.’’
Libby [19] extended the discussed model [105] by replacing Eq.
(78) with Eq. (79) with Kr ¼ Kp ¼ 0.5 and slightly improved the
agreement with the data measured by Moss [125] (cf. solid lines
and symbols in Fig. 30). For ðu02Þbð~cÞ, the agreement between the
measurements and computations was worse, but the difference
between measured and computed values was comparable to the
accuracy of the experiment.
This model was extended by Masuya and Libby [318] to simulate
oblique ﬂames. The extended model contained three more empir-
ical constants (in addition to K1 ¼0.85, K2 ¼ 0.25, and K3 ¼ 0.1). The
simulations showed that the mean pressure gradient term V was
much larger than two sink terms, I and III, and this resulted in an
increase in the ATKE within the ﬂame provided that the heat
release factor was sufﬁciently large (s > 3). It is worth emphasizing,
however, that the increase in ru002 within the ﬂame brush was
mainly controlled by the intermittency. Similarly, term vi in Eq. (21)
was the only term that caused the CGD in the simulated ﬂame,
whereas terms i, iiiþiv, and vii acted in the opposite manner. No
quantitative comparison with experiments was reported.
Chandran and Strahle [319] extended the above model by Bray
et al. [105] by modeling terms v and vi in Eq. (21) together using Eq.
(132) with lF ¼ L and modeling terms IV and V in Eq. (22) using Eq.
(131) with lI ¼ L. No comparison with experiments was reported. As
compared with the original model by Bray et al. [105], the simu-
lations by Chandran and Strahle [319] yielded (i) a monotonic
increase in ru002 with ~c provided that the constants C1 in Eq. (131)
and C1c in Eq. (132) were larger than 2.5, and (ii) the gr

title: Effects of premixed ﬂames on turbulence and turbulent scalar transport
text:
results show that if the angle 4 between the ﬂame and the
upstream ﬂow direction is sufﬁciently small, the production of
ATKE through shear stress exceeds the degradation of ~k due to
dilatation, and the ﬂame may increase the ATKE.
Libby and Bray [100] extended the above model by analyzing
various dependencies of turbulent diffusivity and scalar dissipation
rate on the mean density. For certain dependence, the computed
angle 4 was comparable with the data obtained by Wright and
Zukoski [366] from oblique conﬁned ﬂames stabilized behind
a bluff body. Note that the simulations show a weak dependence of
4 on s in line with the experiments.
Libby and Bray [104] applied Eqs. (16)–(18), (21), and (22) to
a statistically planar, 1D, ﬂame normal to the mean ﬂow. The
pressure terms v in Eq. (21) and IV in Eq. (22) were neglected. The
transport terms ii and II were closed using Eqs. (51) and (52) sup-
plemented with Eqs. (76), (77), and ðu03Þu ¼ ðu03Þb ¼ 0. The dissi-
pation terms iii and iv were modeled using Eq. (102) and the
dissipation term III was modeled using Eq. (101). The reaction term
was closed using Eq. (169) with fn ¼ 5/6. Countergradient scalar
ﬂux was predicted in these simulations.
Bray et al. [105] modiﬁed this model by replacing Eq. (77) with
Eq. (78). By adjusting three model constants, K1 ¼0.85, K2 ¼ 0.25,
and K3 ¼ 0.1, these authors obtained dependencies of ru002ð~cÞ and
ru00c00ð~cÞ which were close to the data measured by Moss [125] (cf.
dashed lines and symbols in Fig. 30). In particular, CGD and an
increase in ru002 with ~c were predicted.
Driscoll and Gulati [243] tested ‘‘exactly the same’’ model by
comparing the proﬁles of ru002ð~cÞ, ru003ð~cÞ, uuð~cÞ, ubð~cÞ, ðu02Þuð~cÞ,
ðu02Þbð~cÞ, ru00c00ð~cÞ, u00ð~cÞ, and ru002c00ð~cÞ computed for a statistically
stationary, planar, 1D ﬂame with the experimental data obtained
from unconﬁned, oblique, methane–air, turbulent ﬂames. For most
proﬁles, the qualitative agreement between the measured and

dicted; (i) values of ru002 in the burned mixture higher than in the
unburned mixture, (ii) an increase in ru002 by s, (iii) the existence of
peak values of ru002 within the ﬂame brush, (iv) an increase in uu
and ub when either ~c or s increases, (v) an increase in ðu02Þu with ~c,
(vi) countergradient scalar ﬂux ru00c00 and an increase in its

agreement was even obtained between certain measured and
computed proﬁles [uuð~cÞ, ðu02Þuð~cÞ, ru00c00ð~cÞ and u00ð~cÞ]. However,
since the measured mean ﬂame brush thickness increased with
distance from the stabilizing rod in the experiments [243], the
agreement between the results of computations of a statistically
stationary, planar, 1D ﬂame with the data measured at a particular
distance (i.e. at a particular stage of ﬂame development) does not
seem to be a reliable validation.

Moreover, the model yielded an increase in ðu02Þb with ~c at
0:1 < ~c < 0:8, whereas the measurements indicated the opposite
trend. To resolve the problem, Driscoll and Gulati [243] measured
all terms in Eqs. (78)–(80) associated with the statistically planar
1D turbulent ﬂame, and found that Eq. (78), which was invoked in
the discussed computations, and Eq. (80) were contradicted by the
experiment, whereas Eq. (79) was ‘‘signiﬁcantly better.’’
Libby [19] extended the discussed model [105] by replacing Eq.
(78) with Eq. (79) with Kr ¼ Kp ¼ 0.5 and slightly improved the
agreement with the data measured by Moss [125] (cf. solid lines
and symbols in Fig. 30). For ðu02Þbð~cÞ, the agreement between the
measurements and computations was worse, but the difference
between measured and computed values was comparable to the
accuracy of the experiment.
This model was extended by Masuya and Libby [318] to simulate
oblique ﬂames. The extended model contained three more empir-
ical constants (in addition to K1 ¼0.85, K2 ¼ 0.25, and K3 ¼ 0.1). The
simulations showed that the mean pressure gradient term V was
much larger than two sink terms, I and III, and this resulted in an
increase in the ATKE within the ﬂame provided that the heat
release factor was sufﬁciently large (s > 3). It is worth emphasizing,
however, that the increase in ru002 within the ﬂame brush was
mainly controlled by the intermittency. Similarly, term vi in Eq. (21)
was the only term that caused the CGD in the simulated ﬂame,
whereas terms i, iiiþiv, and vii acted in the opposite manner. No
quantitative comparison with experiments was reported.
Chandran and Strahle [319] extended the above model by Bray
et al. [105] by modeling terms v and vi in Eq. (21) together using Eq.
(132) with lF ¼ L and modeling terms IV and V in Eq. (22) using Eq.
(131) with lI ¼ L. No comparison with experiments was reported. As
compared with the original model by Bray et al. [105], the simu-
lations by Chandran and Strahle [319] yielde

title: Effects of premixed ﬂames on turbulence and turbulent scalar transport
text:
modeled using Eq. (102). The dissipation term III was modeled using
Eqs. (96) and (103). The reaction term was closed using Eq. (169)
with fn ¼ 5/6. Terms v and IV that involve pressure ﬂuctuations
were calculated using Eq. (125) and Eqs. (111)–(116), respectively.
No comparison with measurements was reported in the cited paper.
Bradley et al. [309] applied Eqs. (16)–(18), (20)–(22) to a statis-
tically planar,1D, expanding, open ﬂame. The transport terms ii and
II were closed using Eqs. (74) and (75) with Cc ¼ 0.15 and Cs ¼ 0.22.
The dissipation terms iii and iv in Eq. (21) were neglected. The
dissipation term III in Eq. (22) was evaluated solving Eq. (96) with
C3 ¼ 0.15, C31 ¼1.44, C32 ¼ 1.92, C33 ¼1.44. Term IV that involve
pressure ﬂuctuations was calculated using Eqs. (111), (114), (118)
with C1 ¼1.8 and C2 ¼ 0. Term v that also involve pressure ﬂuctu-
ations was calculated using Eq. (125) with C1c ¼ 3.0, and C2c ¼ 0. The
reaction term was closed using Eqs. (170)–(173). No straightforward
comparison with measurements are reported in the cited paper, but
the turbulent ﬂame speeds computed using this model agree
reasonably well with the approximation [367] of experimental data
obtained from expanding spherical ﬂames. It is worth noting,
however, that the speeds of planar and spherical ﬂames, with all
other things being equal, should be different due to the substantial
effect of the mean ﬂame brush curvature on ﬂame kernel growth,
discussed elsewhere [368, 369, 370].
Lindstedt and Va´os [315,316] applied Eqs. (16)–(18), (21)–(22) to
simulate experiments by Gulati and Driscoll [242] with an oblique
lean methane–air ﬂame (F ¼ 0.8, u0/SL z 0.58, Ret ¼ 19), by Kostiuk
[371] with a lean propane–air ﬂame stabilized in two opposed jets
(F ¼ 0.9, SL ¼ 0.4 m/s), by Mastorakos [372] with a lean methane–air
ﬂame stabilized in two opposed jets (F ¼ 0.9, SL ¼ 0.32 m/s), and by
Cheng and Shepherd [174] with the stoichiometric ethane-air ﬂame
s9 stabilized in an impinging jet. T

title: Effects of premixed ﬂames on turbulence and turbulent scalar transport
text:
computed proﬁles of u0(x) and v0(x) is also good (see Fig. 31b). For
the experiments by Mastorakos [372], the proﬁles of ~cðxÞ and v0(x)
were well predicted in the simulations, whereas the agreement
between the measured and computed proﬁles of ~uðxÞ and u0(x) was
worse. Certainly the results are impressive, but such a test of the
model against only two sets of experimental data obtained from
very similar ﬂames characterized by low rms turbulent velocities
and by a very low turbulent Reynolds number is deﬁnitely not
sufﬁcient for validating the model.
The simulations of the experiments by Cheng and Shepherd [174]
performed by Lindstedt and Va´os [315] should not be considered to
validate the same model, because (i) constants Cs and Cc were
reduced in these simulations by 20%, (ii) Eq. (96) was replaced with
Eq. (105), but (iii) the effects of these changes on the computed

title: Effects of premixed ﬂames on turbulence and turbulent scalar transport
text:
results were not discussed. The latter simulations were 2D and time-
dependent until steady state was reached. Comparison with the
measured data was straightforward. The measured proﬁles of ~uðxÞ
and ~cðxÞ were well predicted in the simulations. The agreement
between the measured and computed proﬁles of uuð~cÞ and ubð~cÞ
appears to be reasonable (see Fig. 32a), but the model under-
predicted rms velocities within the ﬂame (see Fig. 32b). Although
these results are encouraging, they are not sufﬁcient for thoroughly
validating the particular model used in these simulations. We may
note that the proﬁles of the mean velocity and the mean combustion
progress variable in the ﬂame s9 were also well predicted by
Karmed et al. [313] despite the fact that turbulent scalar ﬂux and
rms velocities were underpredicted in the latter simulations.
Recently, Lindstedt and Va´os [317] simulated highly turbulent
piloted jet ﬂames F1 and F3 (the well-known experiments by Chen
et al. [186]) by solving Eqs. (16), (17), and (22) jointly with
a transport equation for the mass fraction PDF. The Reynolds-stress
Eq. (22) was closed invoking Eqs. (74), (96), and the SSG model
[354] of the pressure terms IV–V. The radial proﬁles of ~k obtained
by Chen et al. [186] from a highly turbulent ﬂame F1 were well
predicted. However, the model substantially (by a factor of three)
overpredicted the ~k obtained from ﬂame F3 characterized by
a lower Reynolds number and, thus, associated with a stronger
inﬂuence of combustion on turbulence. Note, that the closures of
Eq. (22), used by Lindstedt and Va´os in the above three papers
[315–317], differed from one another.
Champion et al. [310–313] applied a model of the effects of
combustion on turbulence and turbulent transport to several
premixed turbulent ﬂames. The present authors are aware of the
following tests of the model; (a) a lean (F ¼ 0.65) propane–air ﬂame
stabilized by a bluff body in a rectangular channel (the Poitiers
experiments [310]), (b) a rich (F ¼ 1.2) propane–air ﬂame stabilized
by a backward-facing step in a rectangular channel (the experi-
ments by Shepherd et al. [257]), (c) a lean (F ¼ 0.89) methane–air
ﬂame stabilized in an impinging jet (the experiments by Li et al.
[227]), and (d) the stoichiometric ethane-air ﬂame stabilized in an
impinging jet (ﬂame s9 investigated by Cheng and Shepherd [174]).
The model by Champion et al. [310–313] involves Eqs. (16)–(18),
(21), and (22). The transport terms ii and II are closed using Eqs. (74)
and (75) with Cs ¼ 0.25 and Cc ¼ 0.18. The dissipation terms iii and iv
in Eq. (21) are modeled using Eq. (102) with K1 ¼1. The viscous
dissipation was computed solving Eq. (96) with C3 ¼ 0.18, C31 ¼1.44,
C32 ¼1.92, and C33 ¼ 1.44. Terms IV and v that involve pressure
ﬂuctuations were calculated using Eqs. (119) and (127), respectively,
with C1 ¼1.8, C2 ¼ 0.6, C3 ¼ 0.3, C1c ¼ 3.0, C2c ¼ 0.5, and C3c ¼ 0.5.
The reaction term was closed using Eq. (169) with fn ¼ 3/4.
Although themodel involves 14constants (Cs,Cc,K1,C3, C31,C32, C33,C1,
C2, C3, C1c, C2c, C3c, and fn), not one of them was changed in the
simulations. However, three different models were used to calculate
the mean reaction rate W in cases (a) and (b), (c), and (d). Moreover,
the two different expressions for W invoked in cases (c) and (d)
were, in fact, empirical.
In case (a), the computed transverse proﬁles of ru002 and rv002
agreed well with the measured ones [311], but this result was
strongly devaluated by the inability of the model to predict the
measured transverse proﬁles of the mean temperature, see Fig. 33.
In case (b), the computed proﬁles of ru002ð~cÞ and ru00c00ð~cÞ agreed
well with the measured ones (see Fig. 34), as well as the axial and
transverse proﬁles of ðu02Þ1=2 [312]. The model underestimated the
mean axial velocity measured at different distances from the ﬂame-
holder, but the differences in the measured and computed u were
comparable with the accuracy of the measurements. The results of
this test are encouraging.
However, the model was not able to yield the CGD [311] docu-
mented in case (c) [227] and also markedly overpredicted ru002 and
rv002 in the ﬂame, whereas the measured proﬁles of ~uðxÞ and ~cðxÞ
were well predicted [311], probably due to the adjustment of the
closure for W.
Similarly, in case (d) also simulated by Lindstedt and Va´os [315],
the model well predicted uðxÞ and cðxÞ, but underpredicted the
Fig. 32. Comparison of the model by Lindstedt and Va´os [316] (lines) and the experiment by Cheng and Shepherd [174] (symbols). (a) Mean velocities conditioned to burned (solid
lines and circles) and unburned (dashed line and squares) mixture vs. the Favre-averaged combustion progress variable. (b) Axial (solid lines and circles) and radial (dashed line and
squares) rms velocities vs. distance from the exit. From the paper by Lindstedt and Va´os [315] 1998 by The Combustion Institute, reproduced with permission.
A.N. Lipatnikov, J. Chomiak / Progress in Energy and Combustion Science 36 (2010) 1–102
53

axial scalar ﬂux, which was countergradient both in the measure-
ments and simulations (see Fig. 35a), and also substantially
underpredicted the axial rms ﬂow velocity (cf. solid line and circles
in Fig. 35b).19
Thus, although the above tests of the model by Champion et al.
[310–313] are encouraging, they do not indicate the ability of the
model to quantitatively predict variations in turbulence charac-
teristics in different premixed ﬂames.
Bray et al. [324] applied Eqs. (16), (17), (21), and (22) to simu-
lating the experiments performed by Li et al. [227] with ﬂames
stabilized in impinging jet ﬂows. Two lean (F ¼ 0.89) methane–air
ﬂames were investigated, with the sole difference between the two
ﬂames consisting of using two different perforated grids in order to
generate turbulence. Since the goal of the simulation was to test the
model of the effects of combustion on turbulence and turbulent
transport, the mean combustion progress variable proﬁle was taken
from measurements, rather than computed.
Eqs. (21) and (22) were closed as follows. The transport terms ii
and II were neglected. The dissipation terms iii and iv were
modeled using Eq. (102). The dissipation term III was modeled
using Eqs. (97) and (103). The pressure term v in Eq. (21) was closed
using Eqs. (144), (145), and (150). The pressure term IV in Eq. (22)
was closed using Eqs. (143)–(148). The reaction term was modeled
using Eq. (169). An important merit of the model consists of
yielding the following analytical expression
ru00c00 ¼ sSLnx~c

1  ~c

(181)
for the turbulent scalar ﬂux in the axial direction.
The numerical model involves seven constants (C31 ¼1.44,
C32 ¼ 1.92, C33 ¼ 0.5, K2 ¼ 0.75, C1 ¼1.8, C2 ¼ 0.6, and fn  K1 ¼0.5)
and three unknown geometric parameters nx, n2x, and n2r . The value
of 0.5 was assigned to the ﬁrst parameter by referring to the DNS by
Domingo and Bray [283]. The two other parameters, n2x ¼ 0:422 and
n2r ¼ 0:075, and constant K2 were adjusted ‘‘to bring the theory and
experiment into agreement’’ [324]. Moreover, two boundary
conditions (the rms velocity in the radial direction and the dissipa-
tion rate at the jet exit) were not known and were selected by
comparing the distributions of the rms velocity in the axial direction,
measured and computed in the reactant stream. These boundary
values were different in the two different ﬂames investigated. Thus,
the model involved ﬁve tuning parameters, at least three of which
were adjusted using results measured within the ﬂames.
The agreement between the model and experiment, reported by
Bray et al. [324], appears to be excellent, see Fig. 36. Later, Bray et al.
[373] wrote that they knew ‘‘no other predictions of turbulence
quantities in these ﬂows that show such good agreement with
experiments.’’ We fully agree with this claim. However, we are not
sure that the obtained agreement is sufﬁcient to validate the model.
First, the model was tested by compari

text:
results were not discussed. The latter simulations were 2D and time-
dependent until steady state was reached. Comparison with the
measured data was straightforward. The measured proﬁles of ~uðxÞ
and ~cðxÞ were well predicted in the simulations. The agreement
between the measured and computed proﬁles of uuð~cÞ and ubð~cÞ
appears to be reasonable (see Fig. 32a), but the model under-
predicted rms velocities within the ﬂame (see Fig. 32b). Although
these results are encouraging, they are not sufﬁcient for thoroughly
validating the particular model used in these simulations. We may
note that the proﬁles of the mean velocity and the mean combustion
progress variable in the ﬂame s9 were also well predicted by
Karmed et al. [313] despite the fact that turbulent scalar ﬂux and
rms velocities were underpredicted in the latter simulations.
Recently, Lindstedt and Va´os [317] simulated highly turbulent
piloted jet ﬂames F1 and F3 (the well-known experiments by Chen
et al. [186]) by solving Eqs. (16), (17), and (22) jointly with
a transport equation for the mass fraction PDF. The Reynolds-stress
Eq. (22) was closed invoking Eqs. (74), (96), and the SSG model
[354] of the pressure terms IV–V. The radial proﬁles of ~k obtained
by Chen et al. [186] from a highly turbulent ﬂame F1 were well
predicted. However, the model substantially (by a factor of three)
overpredicted the ~k obtained from ﬂame F3 characterized by
a lower Reynolds number and, thus, associated with a stronger
inﬂuence of combustion on turbulence. Note, that the closures of
Eq. (22), used by Lindstedt and Va´os in the above three papers
[315–317], differed from one another.
Champion et al. [310–313] applied a model of the effects of
combustion on turbulence and turbulent transport to several
premixed turbulent ﬂames. The present authors are aware of the
following tests of the model; (a) a lean (F ¼ 0.65) propane–air ﬂame
stabilized by a bluff body in a rectangular channel (the Poitiers
experiments [310]), (b) a ric

title: Effects of premixed ﬂames on turbulence and turbulent scalar transport
text:
axial scalar ﬂux, which was countergradient both in the measure-
ments and simulations (see Fig. 35a), and also substantially
underpredicted the axial rms ﬂow velocity (cf. solid line and circles
in Fig. 35b).19
Thus, although the above tests of the model by Champion et al.
[310–313] are encouraging, they do not indicate the ability of the
model to quantitatively predict variations in turbulence charac-
teristics in different premixed ﬂames.
Bray et al. [324] applied Eqs. (16), (17), (21), and (22) to simu-
lating the experiments performed by Li et al. [227] with ﬂames
stabilized in impinging jet ﬂows. Two lean (F ¼ 0.89) methane–air
ﬂames were investigated, with the sole difference between the two
ﬂames consisting of using two different perforated grids in order to
generate turbulence. Since the goal of the simulation was to test the
model of the effects of combustion on turbulence and turbulent
transport, the mean combustion progress variable proﬁle was taken
from measurements, rather than computed.
Eqs. (21) and (22) were closed as follows. The transport terms ii
and II were neglected. The dissipation terms iii and iv were
modeled using Eq. (102). The dissipation term III was modeled
using Eqs. (97) and (103). The pressure term v in Eq. (21) was closed
using Eqs. (144), (145), and (150). The pressure term IV in Eq. (22)
was closed using Eqs. (143)–(148). The reaction term was modeled
using Eq. (169). An important merit of the model consists of
yielding the following analytical expression

The numerical model involves seven constants (C31 ¼1.44,
C32 ¼ 1.92, C33 ¼ 0.5, K2 ¼ 0.75, C1 ¼1.8, C2 ¼ 0.6, and fn  K1 ¼0.5)
and three unknown geometric parameters nx, n2x, and n2r . The value
of 0.5 was assigned to the ﬁrst parameter by referring to the DNS by

title: Effects of premixed ﬂames on turbulence and turbulent scalar transport
text:
within the two ﬂames were close to one another, as well as the
computed proﬁles of Gwc(z). Thus, in fact, the model, which involves
at least three adjustable parameters, was tested using three
substantially different experimental proﬁles. More experimental
data are deﬁnitely required to validate the model.
Second, Fig. 36 is impressive in part because substantial varia-
tions are limited to narrow zones so that the details are not striking.
However, the same data plotted in a different format in Fig. 37
reveal signiﬁcant differences between the model and experiment.
Third, the magnitude of the dissipation rate ~3 computed by Bray
et al. [324] may be put into question, as discussed elsewhere [374].
Finally, Eq. (181) appears to be the most impressive prediction
by the model. This analytical expression involves a single unknown
parameter, the constant value 0.5, which has not been adjusted, but
has been assigned using the DNS data by Domingo and Bray [283].
Thus, Eq. (181) is a real theoretical prediction. Figs. 3c and 4d
plotted by Bray et al. in Ref. [324] validate this prediction and might
be considered to be solid support of the entire model. However, the
original data by Li et al. [227] indicate that the maximum absolute
values of the normalized axial scalar ﬂux differ by 30% (see Fig. 38)
in the two ﬂames, whereas Eq. (181) with a constant nx yields
exactly the same ﬂux. It is therefore unclear why the aforemen-
tioned Figs. 3c and 4d plotted in Ref. [324] show agreement
between Eq. (181) and the measurements in both ﬂames.

method. It needs further development and extensive testing.
For each unclosed term in Eqs. (21) and (22), there are several
competitive models, each involving at least one or even stronger
simpliﬁcations.
Only a
few
such
models
have
been
tested
straightforwardly using DNS databases and the majority of the
tested models have been compared with a single DNS database, e.g.
Eqs. (80) and (81) [270], Eq. (103) [291], Eq. (124) [291], Eq. (125)
[283], Eq. (139) [291], Eqs. (140)–(145) [283], Eq. (149) [283], Eqs.
(164) [278,280]. Very few models have been tested against two
different DNS databases, e.g. Eq. (131) is not supported by the DNS
data obtained by Zhang and Rutland [271] and by Domingo and
Bray [283]. When one DNS database validated a particular model
developed by the authors of the DNS, another database did not
support the same model. For instance, Eq. (163) was validated by
the DNS data obtained by Veynante and Poinsot [131], but did not
agree with the DNS data by Domingo and Bray [283] and by
Chakraborty and Cant [291]. Eq. (106) was validated by Nishiki et al.
[280], but did not agree with the DNS data by Chakraborty and Cant
[291]. Eq. (155) was validated by Domingo and Bray [283], but
overestimated the DNS data by Chakraborty and Cant [291]. The
present authors are aware of only a few exceptions. First, Eq. (134)
was supported not only by the DNS data by Zhang and Rutland
[271], but also by the DNS data by Nishiki et al. [278,279]. Second,
Eq. (164) was supported not only by the DNS data by Nishiki et al.
[278,279], but also by the DNS data by Chakraborty and Cant [291].
Third, Eq. (169) was supported by the DNS data by Nishiki et al.
[278,279] and by Chakraborty and Cant [291].
Thus, we cannot claim that any model of any unclosed term in Eqs.
(21) and (22) is a well-validated model, although certain models
might be claimed to be widely recognized, e.g. Eqs. (74) and (75) for
the transport terms ii and II or Eq. (169) for the reaction term vii.
Various combinations of different submodels for different
unclosed terms in Eqs. (21) and (22) have yielded several net
models, as summarized in Table 3. Since each group uses its own
net model, there is no well-validated net model and only a few net
models have been tested by straightforwardly comparing numer-
ical results with experimental data. The net model by Champion
et al. [310–313] has been tested against a wider set of experimental
data than the competitive net models by Bray et al. [324] and by
Lindstedt and Va´os [315,316]. Although certain tests of the net
model by Champion et al. [310–313] yielded encouraging results
(see Fig. 34), other tests (e.g. Figs. 33 and 35) did not conﬁrm its
predictive capabilities. Two other aforementioned net models,
which were already tested straightforwardly, strongly need vali-
dation against a wider set of representative experimental data
obtained from substantially different ﬂames.

one assumption. Therefore, when testing a net model, the advan-
tages of one submodel may be devaluated by limitations of another
submodel. Similarly, the drawbacks of two different submodels may
cancel one another so that a net model that invokes wrong
assumptions may yield an impressive result. For instance, Bray et al.
[105] well predicted the turbulent scalar ﬂux and rms velocity
measured by Moss [125] in spite of neglecting important terms that
involved the ﬂuctuating pressure gradient. To the contrary, after
extending this net model to account for the neglected terms, Chan-
dran and Strahle [319] were not able to obtain the countergradient
scalar ﬂux documented by Moss [125]. Thus, an improvement of
a particular submodel weakened the net model.
Consequently, studies of the Favre-averaged balance equations
for the second moments, Eqs. (21) and (22), have improved our
understanding of the behavior of various unclosed terms in these
equations, but have not yet led to the development of a model able
to quantitatively predict the effects of combustion on turbulence
and turbulent transport. A few alternative approaches capable of
addressing these effects will be brieﬂy discussed in the next
subsections. The brevity of the following discussion is due to the
fact that there are only just a few applications of alternative
approaches to study the effects referred to.
4.2. Large eddy simulation
Large eddy simulation (LES) is a promising approach to
modeling complex turbulent ﬂows and, in particular, turbulent
ﬂames. In LES, large-scale vortexes are directly calculated, but the
effects of small-scale eddies on ﬂow and ﬂame still require physical
modeling. Although LES is more expensive and difﬁcult than RANS
from
the
computational
viewpoint,
the
former
approach
is
commonly considered to be more accurate for ﬂows where large-
scale unsteady motions play a substantial role. Since major indus-
trial applications of premixed combustion involve such motions
(e.g. the combustion process in reciprocating engines is inherently
transient, while statistically stationary combustion in a gas turbine
engine can become unstable due to thermoacoustic instabilities),
applications of LES to turbulent ﬂames are rapidly growing and
substantial progress has already been obtained in this ﬁeld, as
reviewed elsewhere [49,51].
However, contrary to more expensive DNS, it is difﬁcult to ﬁnd
a positive example of the use of LES for improving our basic
understanding of the interaction between a ﬂame and a turbulent
ﬂow under simple conditions (e.g. in the statistically one-
dimensional case). For this particular task, LES does not seem to be
substantially better than RANS, because ‘‘the essential rate-
controlling processes of molecular mixing and chemical reaction
occur at the smallest scales’’ [375] not resolved in LES. Therefore,
both RANS and LES require models of the same small-scale
phenomena, but, while the models used in RANS have been
developed and tested over several decades, the models used in LES
are new and strongly need validation. In fact, the vast majority of
combustion models used in LES have been recast using RANS
models. The state of premixed combustion modeling for LES
resembles the state of closing terms II–IV in Eq. (22) for RANS of
turbulent ﬂames. There are a number of competitive models, each
research group develops its own, but no well validated and widely
recognized approach has yet emerged.
Thus, LES has not yet contributed notably to understanding the
effects of premixed combustion on turbulence and only very few
LESs have addressed these effects.
Boger et al. [274] processed a DNS database (see Table 2) in order
to evaluate unresolved turbulent scalar ﬂux in LES. Both the CGD
and the GD directions of the unresolved ﬂux were documented
depending on the value of s, with the large values promoting the
CGD direction. This result conﬁrmed that the problem of modeling
pressure-driven turbulent transport is relevant not only to RANS
but also to LES. However, Boger et al. [274] pointed out that (i) the
unresolved ﬂux in the LES was substantially lower than ru00c00=r in
RANS, because a part of the latter ﬂux was directly resolved in the
LES and, hence, (ii) uncertainties in modeling the LES-ﬂux would
‘‘have probably less dramatic consequences than in RANS.’’ By
processing a 2D DNS database obtained for a V-shaped ﬂame (see
Table 2), Domingo et al. [295] conﬁrmed that the unresolved
streamwise scalar ﬂux associated with LES was low (Crvfcf D/
<rvc > z 0.04, where CaD is a ﬁltered value of a quantity a and
<r>af ¼ <ra><r><a>). However, the unresolved ﬂux in the
transverse direction associated with the normal to the mean ﬂame
brush was countergradient and not negligible. It was approximately
equal to CrvcD. Nevertheless, Domingo et al. [295] have hypothesized
that ‘‘the modeling of turbulent transport in premixed ﬂames may
be easier to handle with LES than with RANS,’’ because the transport
terms in the ﬁltered balance equation for the combustion progress
variable are much lower than other terms in this equation. We may
note, however, that the transport term A in the Favre-averaged Eq.
(18) may be much smaller than other terms in that equation (e.g.
see DNS by Rutland and Cant [270], by Trouve´ et al. [127], and by
Veynante and Poinsot [131], discussed in Section 4.1.1).

measured by Chen et al. [186] near the burner exit, but mark-

 Selle et al. [390] and Roux et al. [391] applied the thickened
ﬂame model, developed by Colin et al. [376], to the LES of two
industrial gas turbine burners and reported a good agreement
(see Figs. 24–25 in Ref. [390] and Figs. 21–23 in Ref. [391])
between the computed and measured rms velocities.
 Domingo et al. [295] developed a FSD-PDF subgrid scale closure
for the LES of premixed turbulent combustion and well pre-
dicted (see Fig. 22 in the cited paper) the transverse proﬁles of
rms velocities measured by Nguyen and Bruel [392] in a lean
(F ¼ 0.75) premixed ﬂame stabilized in a dump combustor
(u0 z 2 m/s).
 Duwig and Fuchs [393] applied a ‘‘ﬁltered ﬂamelet technique’’
to a gas turbine burner, which looked similar to a dump
combustor (see Fig. 2 in the cited paper), and reasonably well
predicted (see Figs. 5 and 6 therein) the measured transverse
proﬁles of u0 and v0 for a lean (F ¼ 0.4) preheated methane–air
mixture.
 Nogenmyr et al. [394] applied a G-equation approach to the LES
of a lean (F ¼ 0.62) methane–air ﬂame stabilized in a low-swirl
ﬂow and well-predicted (see Figs. 3 and 4 in the cited paper)
the measured rms velocities within the ﬂame (u0/SL z 5).
 Freitag and Janicka [395] applied a different G-equation
approach to the LES of a lean (F ¼ 0.833) methane–air ﬂame
stabilized in a strongly swirled ﬂow and well-predicted (see
Fig. 8 in the cited paper) the radial proﬁles of the ATKE
measured by Schneider et al. [239] (u0/SL z 3).
 Duwig and Fuchs [396] applied a marker ﬁeld approach [400]
to the LES of the aforementioned experiments by Pitz and
Daily [383]. The measured transverse proﬁles of streamwise
rms velocity (see Fig. 3 in the cited paper) were well predicted
in the vicinity of the rearward-facing step used to stabilize the
ﬂame, but were substantially overpredicted at far distances
(x/h ¼ 7, where h is the step height).
A.N. Lipatnikov, J. Chomiak / Progress in Energy and Combustion Science 36 (2010) 1–102
58

 Duwig [397] combined the aforementioned ﬁltered ﬂamelet
technique with the fractal ﬂame-wrinkling model20 developed
by Fureby [398], then applied this combined approach to the
LES of the aforementioned experiments by Nguyen and Bruel
[392], and reported reasonable agreement between measured
and computed u0, v0, and u0v0 in the vicinity of ﬂame-stabiliza-
tion zone. ‘‘Further downstream,. the LES over-estimates the
ﬂuctuation levels’’ [397].
Even if this list is far from a complete list of successive applica-
tions of LES to predicting rms velocities measured in premixed
turbulent ﬂames, it is encouraging. However, it also raises questions.
First, why have a number of substantially different combustion
models all been claimed to well predict experimental data? The
history of science has shown that there is one ‘‘true’’ model of
a physical process and a number of wrong models. If a number of
models have all been claimed to be ‘‘true’’, as in the case considered,
the sole conclusion we can draw is that many more tests must be
conducted to rank the aforementioned LES combustion models.
Indeed, most of them have been tested using only a single set of

title: Effects of premixed ﬂames on turbulence and turbulent scalar transport
text:
experimental data of Cho et al. [223,224] and Cheng and
Shepherd [174].
 Pitsch and de Lageneste [389] applied a G-equation model to
the LES of a stoichiometric methane–air Bunsen ﬂame F3
stabilized by a large pilot ﬂame and well predicted (see Fig. 6 in
the
cited
paper)
the
apparent
turbulent
kinetic
energy
measured by Chen et al. [186] near the burner exit, but mark-
edly overpredicted the ATKE at a larger distance from the exit.
 Selle et al. [390] and Roux et al. [391] applied the thickened
ﬂame model, developed by Colin et al. [376], to the LES of two
industrial gas turbine burners and reported a good agreement
(see Figs. 24–25 in Ref. [390] and Figs. 21–23 in Ref. [391])
between the computed and measured rms velocities.
 Domingo et al. [295] developed a FSD-PDF subgrid scale closure
for the LES of premixed turbulent combustion and well pre-
dicted (see Fig. 22 in the cited paper) the transverse proﬁles of
rms velocities measured by Nguyen and Bruel [392] in a lean
(F ¼ 0.75) premixed ﬂame stabilized in a dump combustor
(u0 z 2 m/s).
 Duwig and Fuchs [393] applied a ‘‘ﬁltered ﬂamelet technique’’
to a gas turbine burner, which looked similar to a dump
combustor (see Fig. 2 in the cited paper), and reasonably well
predicted (see Figs. 5 and 6 therein) the measured transverse
proﬁles of u0 and v0 for a lean (F ¼ 0.4) preheated methane–air
mixture.
 Nogenmyr et al. [394] applied a G-equation approach to the LES
of a lean (F ¼ 0.62) methane–air ﬂame stabilized in a low-swirl
ﬂow and well-predicted (see Figs. 3 and 4 in the cited paper)
the measured rms velocities within the ﬂame (u0/SL z 5).
 Freitag and Janicka [395] applied a different G-equation
approach to the LES of a lean (F ¼ 0.833) methane–air ﬂame
stabilized in a strongly swirled ﬂow and well-predicted (see
Fig. 8 in the cited paper) the radial proﬁles of the ATKE
measured by Schneider et al. [239] (u0/SL z 3).
 Duwig and Fuchs [396] applied a marker ﬁeld approach [400]
to the LES of the aforemention

title: Effects of premixed ﬂames on turbulence and turbulent scalar transport
text:
 Duwig [397] combined the aforementioned ﬁltered ﬂamelet
technique with the fractal ﬂame-wrinkling model20 developed
by Fureby [398], then applied this combined approach to the
LES of the aforementioned experiments by Nguyen and Bruel
[392], and reported reasonable agreement between measured
and computed u0, v0, and u0v0 in the vicinity of ﬂame-stabiliza-
tion zone. ‘‘Further downstream,. the LES over-estimates the
ﬂuctuation levels’’ [397].
Even if this list is far from a complete list of successive applica-
tions of LES to predicting rms velocities measured in premixed
turbulent ﬂames, it is encouraging. However, it also raises questions.
First, why have a number of substantially different combustion
models all been claimed to well predict experimental data? The
history of science has shown that there is one ‘‘true’’ model of
a physical process and a number of wrong models. If a number of
models have all been claimed to be ‘‘true’’, as in the case considered,
the sole conclusion we can draw is that many more tests must be
conducted to rank the aforementioned LES combustion models.
Indeed, most of them have been tested using only a single set of

title: Effects of premixed ﬂames on turbulence and turbulent scalar transport
text:
experimental data.21
Second, why has the aforementioned LES well predicted rms
velocities in various premixed ﬂames? The rationale for this ques-
tion is as follows: The small-scale processes conﬁned to the
instantaneous ﬂame front were not resolved and the inﬂuence of
the front on turbulence was not modeled in these LES. However,
processes such as unburned–burned intermittency, dissipation
within the ﬂame front, the ﬂame-front contribution to the ﬂuctu-
ating velocity–pressure gradient correlation, etc. are well known to
substantially affect turbulence in ﬂames. This issue deﬁnitely needs
clarifying.
A tentative answer to the latter question could be as follows: The
majority of the above tests [295,382,384,386,389–391,396] were
performed using experimental data obtained from highly turbulent
ﬂames characterized by u0/SL ¼ O(10). Probably, the effects of the
instantaneous ﬂame front on ﬂow are of minor importance in such
a strong turbulence, and the LES that ignore these effects can well
predict experimental data. Further studies of this issue are deﬁ-
nitely necessary.
All in all, although LES is a very promising and rapidly devel-
oping numerical approach, which will probably dominate in the
foreseeable future, it has not yet matured into a tool capable of
predicting turbulence characteristics in premixed ﬂames associated
with the substantial inﬂuence of combustion on turbulence. The
successful applications of LES to various premixed turbulent ﬂames
are encouraging, but are only a ﬁrst step, rather than a ﬁnal one. It
seems too optimistic to believe that LES is a universal predictive
tool until subgrid models of the aforementioned unresolved, small-
scale phenomena are well elaborated.
To conclude this subsection, it is worth stressing that we have
not tried to present even a brief review of the LES of premixed
turbulent ﬂames. The purpose of this subsection was simply to
show that the physical modeling (i.e. discussing physical mecha-
nisms and analyzing unclosed terms) of the effects of premixed
combustion on turbulence is still beyond the mainstream LES.
4.3. Balance equation for probability density function
A transport equation for the joint mass density PDF rP(t, x, u, c)
of the ﬂow velocity u(t, x) and the combustion progress variable
c(t, x) is another alternative tool for modeling turbulent combus-
tion, as reviewed elsewhere [3, 4, 9, 20, 21]. When simulating the
effects of premixed ﬂames on turbulence, in particular an impor-
tant merit of this approach consists of the opportunity to
straightforwardly determine the third moments ru00
i u00
j u00
k, ru00
i u00
j c00
and the reaction term vii in Eq. (21) using rP(t, x, u, c) without
invoking additional model assumptions. Nevertheless, due to
difﬁculties in modeling unclosed terms and performing numerical
integration in multi-dimensional space (the PDF depends on eight
variables in a general unsteady 3D case and on ﬁve variables, u1, u2,
u3, c, and x, in the simplest statistically stationary, planar, 1D case),
there have been few attempts to study the discussed effects by
solving the transport equation for the joint PDF, and these studies
are restricted to a statistically planar 1D ﬂame.
Anand and Pope [320] simulated a statistically stationary, 1D,
planar ﬂame by using stochastic mixing and stochastic reor-
ientation models to close terms in the transport equation for the
joint mass density PDF that are associated with molecular viscosity
and ﬂuctuating pressure gradient. ‘‘The combined effect of the
stochastic models on the Reynolds stresses is equivalent to that of
Rotta’s return-to-isotropy model’’ [320], i.e. the two stochastic
models together yield a term similar to F11
(1) given by Eq. (114) in the
balance equation for the ATKE derived from the transport equation
for the joint PDF. Similarly, the two stochastic models together yield
the term C1cru00c00ð~3=~kÞ [cf. Eq. (125) with C2c ¼ 0] in the balance
equation for ru00c00. The simulations were performed using the
Monte Carlo method [20].
Anand and Pope [320] compared their numerical results with
the data measured by Moss [125] and computed early by Bray et al.
[105] and by Libby [19] using the BML model. All these approaches
have been claimed [320] to yield very similar distributions of ubð~cÞ
and uuð~cÞ in spite of several particular differences between the
models (e.g. the ﬂuctuating velocity–pressure gradient terms v and

BML model overestimated ru02ð~cÞ in comparison with the model by
Anand and Pope [320].
Hulek and Lindstedt [350] applied the joint PDF transport
equation and the Monte Carlo method to study both transient and
fully developed, statistically 1D, planar premixed turbulent ﬂames.
The molecular viscosity and ﬂuctuating pressure gradient terms in
the transport equation were closed invoking two alternative
models; either the generalized Langevin model by Haworth and
Pope [351,352] or the realizable Lagrangian model (LSSG), i.e. the
SSG model of Speziale, Sarkar, and Gatski [354] modiﬁed by Pope
[353]. It is worth recalling that the redistribution terms in Eq. (137)
are associated with the former model.

computed and measured results was claimed to be ‘‘excellent’’
[350] (see Fig. 3 in the cited paper), and both the Langevin and the
20 This model invokes an outdated empirical parameterization to calculate the
fractal dimension D. This parameterization is contradicted by recent experimental
data, see, e.g. Fig. 3 in Refs. [197,199,302,399]. It is also worth noting that experi-
mentally determined D is very sensitive to the method used to process ﬂame-front
images. For instance, Cintosun et al. [199] processed the same images using three
different methods (the caliper technique, the box-counting method, and Minkowski
circle technique) and obtained D¼2.2, 2.32 and 2.4, respectively. Although, the
difference in D¼2.2 and 2.4 may appear to be small, this difference is quite
substantial, because the fractal models involve a power exponent D  2 ¼ 0:2 and
0.4, respectively. Due to this large difference, the use of the fractal dimension as an
input parameter known from measurements offers ample opportunity for tuning.
21 Moreover, sometimes, the same research group uses different combustion
models to perform LES of different premixed turbulent ﬂames. For instance, Duwig
and Fuchs [393,396,397] applied three different combustion models to three
different ﬂames and reported reasonable agreement with measurements each time.

LSSG models yielded very similar St. For the propane–air ﬂame, the
model well predicted the experimental data at u0/SL < 5, but
markedly overpredicted a ratio of St/SL at higher u0/SL (see Fig. 5 in
Ref. [350]). The latter disagreement was associated with local ﬂame
quenching by strong turbulence in the experiments.
From our viewpoint, the above test disproves, rather than vali-
dates the model. First, the speed of an expanding statistically
spherical turbulent ﬂame is substantially reduced due to the
curvature of the mean ﬂame brush as compared with a statistically
planar ﬂame, all other things being equal [368–370]. Under typical
conditions of the Leeds experiments, the magnitude of the effect
can be as large as 200–300% [369,370].
Second, the ﬂames investigated by Abdel-Gayed et al. [401,402]
were developing ﬂames, rather than fully developed ones. Hulek
and Lindstedt [350] have claimed that ‘‘a correction of the experi-
mental data to account for a fully developed turbulent ﬂame results
in

and Lindstedt [350] have claimed that ‘‘a correction of the experi-
mental data to account for a fully developed turbulent ﬂame results

ﬂame development does not seem to be fully consistent with the
computed data. On page 489, Hulek and Lindstedt [350] report that
the computed thickness of the methane–air ﬂame brush is about
ten times larger than the turbulence length scale. Even if we
assume that L ¼ 20 mm following Hulek and Lindstedt [350]
(Abdel-Gayed et al. [401, 402] reported that L z 40 mm), then, the
computed ﬂame brush is still larger than the radius (152.5 mm) of
the bomb used by Abdel-Gayed et al. [401, 402] and the maximum
possible radius of the ﬂame kernel (60 mm), i.e. the half distance
between two identical spark plugs that simultaneously ignited the
mixture in the measurements.

title: Effects of premixed ﬂames on turbulence and turbulent scalar transport
text:
In all the ﬂames simulated in Ref. [408], these two terms, i.e. the
ﬁrst and second terms on the RHS of Eq. (233), were much less than
the three other terms on the RHS of this equation. Therefore, the
obtained results were weakly sensitive to variations in C2c and/or u0
in Eq. (240).
Figs. 42 and 43 summarize the results of testing the conditioned
balance Eqs. (215)–(220), closed using Eqs. (230), (231), (234), (235),
and
(240),
against

title: Effects of premixed ﬂames on turbulence and turbulent scalar transport
text:
experimental
data
obtained
from
seven
impinging jet ﬂames by four research groups [174,224,227,228]. It is
worth stressing that no model constant or input parameter that was
directly relevant to the tested balance equations was tuned to
improve the agreement between the measured and computed data
shown in Figs. 42 and 43. Indeed, Eq. (234) did not involve any
constant or unknown input parameter. The constant 2/3 on the RHS
of Eq. (235) was set based on other experimental data obtained from
other ﬂames beforethe simulations.The computed results were only
weakly sensitive to variations in C2,c and u0 in Eq. (240). Therefore,
Figs. 42 and 43 allow us to claim that the performed tests indicate the
predictive capabilities of the conditioned balance equations.
Certainly, one single encouraging test is not sufﬁcient to consider
the conditioned balance Eqs. (215)–(220) to be a breakthrough. This
new approach deﬁnitely needs further study and applications to
various ﬂames. For instance, the above tests do not allow us to assess
the used closure of the difference in the conditioned pressure
gradients, Eqs. (239) or (240), because the counterpart term on the
RHS of Eq. (233) was small in the simulated ﬂames. Moreover, the
ability of the approach to predict the conditioned Reynolds stresses
in ﬂames has not yet been demonstrated. Nevertheless, the ﬁrst test
[408] appears to be sufﬁcient to consider the above conditioned
balance equations as a promisingapproach.
4.5. Simple models of turbulent scalar ﬂux
The models discussed in the above part of Section 4 consider
balance equations (either for second-order moments, or for the
joint PDF P(u, c), or for ﬁltered quantities, or for conditioned
velocities and Reynolds stresses). Discussed in this subsection are
two much simpler models proposed to evaluate scalar ﬂuxes in
premixed turbulent ﬂames.
4.5.1. Model by Chomiak and Nisbet [334]
Chomiak and Nisbet [334] have decomposed turbulent scalar
ﬂux as follows
